refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure and report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined target. The latter is a success criterion dependent on operational execution, not a system requirement. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—providing visibility into throughput—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of whether a specific productivity target is met. The target itself belongs in acceptance criteria or KPI definitions, not in the FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports material throughput (m³/h or ton/h) during representative operations. Verify data accuracy against independent measurement. Confirm that reported metrics are accessible to Site Manager and System Engineer stakeholders in real time or near-real time.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-001.1: Acquire material throughput data (volume or mass per time interval) during equipment operation.
FR-NGHE-001.2: Store and log throughput measurements with associated timestamps and operational context.
FR-NGHE-001.3: Report throughput metrics to authorized stakeholders in a human-readable format.

candidate_dps: DP1: Continuous volumetric flow measurement via inline sensor with real-time signal conditioning and local data buffering
DP2: Periodic mass-based sampling with calibrated weighing and extrapolation to throughput rate over fixed time windows
DP3: Hybrid dual-path measurement (volumetric primary + mass-based verification) with cross-validation logic to detect sensor drift or anomalies
DP4: Indirect throughput inference from process parameters (e.g., pump speed, conveyor velocity, material density lookup) with empirical calibration curve
DP5: Decoupled measurement pipeline: acquire raw sensor data → normalize to standard conditions → apply material-specific conversion factors → aggregate and report at configurable intervals
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Decoupled measurement pipeline with material-agnostic data acquisition, normalization, and configurable reporting

potential_sources: Tight coupling between sensor selection and conversion logic if material properties are hardcoded into signal conditioning
Reporting interval locked to sensor sampling rate rather than independently configurable
Normalization assumptions (temperature, pressure, density) embedded in DP rather than externalized as parameters
coupling_check.notes: DP5 achieves low coupling by separating concerns: (1) raw acquisition is sensor-agnostic, (2) normalization is parameterized by material type and environmental conditions, (3) reporting cadence is independent of measurement frequency. This allows swapping measurement modalities (volumetric ↔ mass) or adjusting reporting granularity without redesign. Coupling risk remains if material properties or environmental corrections are not externalized as configuration.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Material properties (density, compressibility, moisture content) are stable or measurable during evaluation period. Risk: Variability in material properties invalidates conversion factors; mitigation requires periodic recalibration or dual-path verification (DP3).

Assumption: Sensor accuracy and drift are within acceptable bounds over the evaluation period. Risk: Undetected sensor drift causes reported throughput to diverge from true productivity; mitigation requires periodic calibration checkpoints or cross-validation.

Assumption: 'Productivity target' is defined in consistent units (mass/time or volume/time) and environmental conditions are specified (e.g., standard temperature, pressure for gas-phase materials). Risk: Ambiguity in target definition leads to acceptance disputes; clarify units and reference conditions in acceptance criteria.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan permits installation of measurement instrumentation without disrupting material flow or creating hazards. Risk: Intrusive sensors may alter flow characteristics or create maintenance/cleaning burdens; non-intrusive alternatives (e.g., radar, ultrasonic) may be required.

Assumption: Data buffering and reporting infrastructure can tolerate network latency or temporary connectivity loss without losing measurement integrity. Risk: Dropped data or reporting delays mask transient throughput variations; design must include local persistence and reconciliation logic.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) measurement units (mass per unit time or volume per unit time, and reference conditions if applicable), (2) required measurement accuracy and acceptable sensor drift tolerance, (3) reporting frequency and latency requirements, (4) whether material properties are known/constant or must be inferred, and (5) constraints on sensor placement and intrusiveness imposed by safety plan. Recommend pairing DP5 with DP3 (hybrid verification) if high confidence in productivity reporting is critical to acceptance; this trades implementation complexity for robustness against single-point sensor failures.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized measurement pipeline orchestration, material-agnostic data acquisition abstraction, normalization logic, and configurable reporting generation

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution emits normalized measurement data and reporting outputs to external telemetry consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services)

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution receives measurement configuration, reporting format directives, and pipeline control commands from tasking authority

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution may communicate with remote data sources or reporting endpoints via network if distributed acquisition is required

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the logical home for material-agnostic measurement abstraction, normalization, and configurable reporting. The decoupled pipeline architecture aligns with NGHE's role as a solution-level orchestrator. Minor diffusion: raw sensor acquisition may physically reside in Vehicle; reporting consumption occurs in downstream consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services).

Poor fit. Vehicle is a physical platform, not a logical measurement pipeline. While Vehicle may host sensors and actuators, the decoupled, material-agnostic normalization and configurable reporting logic would diffuse across Vehicle and external systems. Violates separation of concerns: vehicle-specific hardware should not couple to abstract measurement logic.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a control/communication interface, not a measurement pipeline. Placing measurement normalization and reporting here would create tight coupling between remote operation logic and data acquisition, reducing reusability and testability. High diffusion: measurement data would need to flow back to Vehicle and Safety Services.

Poor fit. Safety Services is a specialized consumer of measurement data, not the producer/normalizer. Placing the pipeline here would make safety logic responsible for all measurement abstraction, violating single responsibility. High diffusion: non-safety measurement consumers (Teleoperation, Vehicle diagnostics) would depend on Safety Services.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP5 (decoupled measurement pipeline with material-agnostic data acquisition, normalization, and configurable reporting) is best contained within NGHE Solution because: (1) NGHE Solution is the system-level orchestrator responsible for cross-cutting concerns like data abstraction and reporting; (2) material-agnostic normalization is a logical, reusable service that should not be embedded in domain-specific modules (Vehicle, Safety Services, Teleoperation); (3) configurable reporting is a meta-level capability that benefits all consumers when centralized; (4) decoupling is achieved by exposing normalized data via Telemetry interface, allowing Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services to consume without coupling to acquisition details.
containment_risks: Risk: Raw sensor acquisition may physically reside in Vehicle hardware, requiring a clear interface contract (e.g., via Tasking) for NGHE Solution to configure and read raw data. Mitigation: define sensor abstraction layer in NGHE Solution that Vehicle implements.

Risk: Reporting consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) may attempt to bypass normalization and request raw data, creating diffusion. Mitigation: enforce Telemetry interface as the sole data export path; document that raw data access requires explicit justification.

Risk: Material-specific normalization rules may proliferate if not carefully versioned and maintained in NGHE Solution. Mitigation: establish a configuration schema and version control for normalization rules; treat as part of NGHE Solution's core IP.

Risk: Performance bottleneck if NGHE Solution becomes a centralized measurement hub for high-frequency data. Mitigation: design pipeline with streaming/batching options; allow edge normalization in Vehicle if latency is critical, but keep configuration and reporting logic in NGHE Solution.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 (decoupled measurement pipeline with material-agnostic data acquisition, normalization, and configurable reporting) directly and sufficiently addresses FR (enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time). The DP explicitly covers data acquisition abstraction, normalization logic, and configurable reporting generation—all necessary to satisfy the functional requirement. Material-agnostic design ensures applicability across different material types and measurement units.

diffusion_risks: Raw sensor acquisition interface: Vehicle hardware will physically host sensors; NGHE Solution must define and enforce a clear sensor abstraction contract via Tasking interface to avoid implicit coupling. Without explicit schema, Vehicle may expose hardware-specific details that leak into normalization logic.

Reporting consumer bypass: Teleoperation, Vehicle diagnostics, and Safety Services may attempt direct sensor access or request raw data, circumventing normalization. Mitigation requires strict Telemetry interface governance and documented justification process for exceptions.

Material-specific normalization rule proliferation: If normalization rules are not centrally versioned and maintained in NGHE Solution, different consumers may implement divergent rules, creating silent data inconsistency. Establish configuration schema and version control as non-negotiable.

Performance bottleneck at centralized hub: High-frequency sensor streams (e.g., continuous volumetric flow measurement) may create latency or throughput bottlenecks in NGHE Solution. Mitigation: design pipeline with streaming/batching; allow edge normalization in Vehicle only if latency is critical, but keep reporting configuration and meta-logic in NGHE Solution to preserve single source of truth.

Network Link dependency: If distributed acquisition is required, Network Link introduces external failure modes (latency, packet loss, availability). Ensure graceful degradation and local caching strategies are defined in NGHE Solution design.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct ownership boundary (containment_score 0.92, diffusion_score 0.08). The decoupled pipeline architecture aligns with NGHE's role as solution-level orchestrator. Material-agnostic normalization and configurable reporting are cross-cutting concerns that must not be embedded in domain-specific modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). The three external interfaces (Telemetry out, Tasking in, Network Link inout) are well-defined and support clean separation: Vehicle and downstream consumers depend on NGHE Solution for normalized data, not vice versa. Minor diffusion is acceptable and expected: raw sensor hardware resides in Vehicle; reporting consumption occurs in downstream systems. This is architectural layering, not diffusion failure.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Acceptance criteria (reported productivity ≥ productivity_target over evaluation period) is directly verifiable via NGHE Solution's configurable reporting interface. Telemetry output can be logged and compared against target. However, verification requires: (1) clear definition of productivity_target (volumetric or mass units per unit time); (2) specification of evaluation period duration and sampling frequency; (3) agreement on which material types and measurement units are in scope. Recommend adding explicit acceptance criteria: 'Reported throughput values shall match ground-truth calibrated measurements within ±X% over representative material types and flow rates.'
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns measurement pipeline orchestration, data acquisition abstraction, normalization, and reporting generation. Vehicle owns raw sensor hardware and implements sensor abstraction layer per NGHE contract. Teleoperation and Safety Services are consumers only. Worksite safety plan and operational policies are external constraints that should inform normalization rules (e.g., safety-critical thresholds) but do not change module ownership. Recommend documenting: (1) sensor abstraction interface specification (Tasking direction); (2) normalized data schema (Telemetry direction); (3) configuration versioning and approval process for normalization rules.

recommended_revisions: Add explicit sensor abstraction layer specification: define the interface contract between Vehicle (sensor provider) and NGHE Solution (consumer) via Tasking. Include schema for raw sensor metadata (sensor type, calibration, units, frequency) and configuration directives (sampling rate, filtering, thresholds).

Specify normalized data schema in Telemetry interface: define the canonical format for throughput measurements (e.g., JSON with timestamp, material_type, volume_or_mass, unit, confidence_interval). Enforce this as the sole data export path; document that raw data access requires explicit exception approval.

Establish normalization rule versioning and governance: treat material-specific normalization rules as configuration artifacts with version control, change approval, and audit trail. Store in NGHE Solution; allow dynamic loading via Tasking to support new material types without code changes.

Define performance and latency requirements: specify acceptable latency for measurement-to-reporting pipeline (e.g., <100ms for safety-critical thresholds, <5s for productivity reporting). If high-frequency streams exceed NGHE Solution's centralized capacity, design edge normalization in Vehicle with NGHE Solution retaining reporting and meta-logic ownership.

Add network resilience strategy: if Network Link is used for distributed acquisition, define fallback behavior (local caching, graceful degradation, retry logic) to ensure measurement continuity during network outages.

Clarify acceptance criteria: specify productivity_target value, evaluation period duration, sampling frequency, and acceptable measurement uncertainty (±X%). Include representative material types and flow rate ranges to be validated during gate review.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence (containment_score 0.92, verification_feasibility confirmed). However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks reveal that DP5 is logically sound but architecturally incomplete at this level. The decoupled measurement pipeline requires explicit sub-level decomposition to address: (1) sensor abstraction layer specification (Tasking interface contract), (2) normalized data schema definition (Telemetry interface contract), (3) normalization rule governance and versioning, (4) performance/latency requirements and edge vs. centralized trade-offs, and (5) network resilience strategy. These are not design flaws but rather architectural details that must be pushed down to the next decomposition level to ensure implementability, interface clarity, and risk mitigation. Safety criticality (high) further mandates explicit sub-FR decomposition to isolate safety-critical measurement thresholds and their verification pathways. Proceeding to sub-level decomposition will transform DP5 from a conceptual solution into a detailed, implementable architecture with clear ownership boundaries and interface contracts.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Acquire raw sensor data from Vehicle hardware with abstraction of sensor type, calibration metadata, and measurement units via standardized Tasking interface contract.
FR5.2: Normalize raw sensor measurements to canonical throughput units (volumetric or mass per unit time) using material-specific rules with version control and dynamic configuration loading.

FR5.3: Generate configurable throughput reports (productivity metrics, safety-critical thresholds, audit trails) in standardized Telemetry schema for downstream consumers (Teleoperation, Safety Services, diagnostics).

FR5.4: Ensure measurement pipeline latency and throughput meet performance requirements (safety-critical thresholds <100ms, productivity reporting <5s) with graceful degradation during Network Link outages.

FR5.5: Maintain single source of truth for normalization rules and reporting configuration with version control, change approval audit trail, and support for dynamic material type addition without code changes.

revise.why: DP5 is conceptually correct but requires explicit sub-DP articulation to address the six recommended_revisions. Specifically: (1) sensor abstraction layer DP must define Tasking interface schema (raw sensor metadata, configuration directives); (2) normalization DP must specify canonical Telemetry schema and rule versioning mechanism; (3) performance DP must allocate latency budgets and define edge vs. centralized trade-off criteria; (4) resilience DP must specify Network Link fallback behavior. These sub-DPs will transform DP5 from a logical solution into an implementable architecture with clear interface contracts and risk mitigation strategies. Recommend revising DP5 summary to explicitly reference these sub-level design parameters once decomposition is complete.

assumptions: Sensor abstraction layer can be enforced via Tasking interface contract without requiring Vehicle code changes for each new sensor type.
Normalization rules can be externalized as configuration artifacts and dynamically loaded by NGHE Solution without performance degradation.
Network Link is available for distributed acquisition but may experience transient outages; local caching and graceful degradation are acceptable fallback strategies.
Productivity_target, evaluation period, and acceptable measurement uncertainty (±X%) will be specified during acceptance criteria refinement.
Safety-critical measurement thresholds (e.g., overfill detection, flow rate limits) are distinct from productivity reporting and require separate latency/reliability budgets.
open_risks: Sensor abstraction contract enforcement: Vehicle hardware may expose implementation-specific details (e.g., raw ADC values, hardware-specific calibration formats) that leak into normalization logic if Tasking interface is not rigorously specified and validated. Mitigation: define Tasking schema with examples and validation tests before Vehicle implementation begins.

Normalization rule proliferation and divergence: Without centralized versioning and strict governance, different consumers (Teleoperation, Safety Services, diagnostics) may implement divergent normalization rules, creating silent data inconsistency. Mitigation: establish configuration schema, version control, and change approval process as non-negotiable architectural constraints.

Centralized pipeline bottleneck: High-frequency sensor streams (e.g., continuous volumetric flow measurement at >100Hz) may exceed NGHE Solution's centralized processing capacity, introducing latency or packet loss. Mitigation: design sub-DP for edge normalization in Vehicle with NGHE Solution retaining reporting and meta-logic ownership; define latency budget allocation.

Network Link dependency and resilience: If distributed acquisition is required, Network Link introduces external failure modes (latency, packet loss, availability). Mitigation: define fallback behavior (local caching, graceful degradation, retry logic) in sub-DP; specify acceptable measurement gaps and recovery time.

Reporting consumer bypass: Teleoperation, Vehicle diagnostics, and Safety Services may attempt direct sensor access or request raw data, circumventing normalization. Mitigation: document strict Telemetry interface governance and establish explicit exception approval process with audit trail.

Safety-critical threshold verification: Acceptance criteria must distinguish between productivity reporting (±X% tolerance acceptable) and safety-critical thresholds (e.g., overfill detection, flow rate limits) which may require tighter tolerance and independent verification. Mitigation: decompose FR5 to isolate safety-critical measurement sub-FRs and define separate acceptance criteria.
evidence_needed: Tasking interface specification: define raw sensor metadata schema (sensor_type, calibration_date, calibration_factor, units, frequency, confidence_interval) and configuration directives (sampling_rate, filtering_type, thresholds) with examples for at least 3 representative sensor types (volumetric flow meter, mass scale, load cell).

Telemetry schema specification: define canonical throughput measurement format (JSON or protobuf) with timestamp, material_type, volume_or_mass, unit, confidence_interval, and metadata (sensor_id, normalization_rule_version, evaluation_period). Include examples for volumetric and mass-based measurements.

Normalization rule versioning and governance process: document how material-specific rules are stored, versioned, approved, and deployed. Include change control procedure, audit trail, and rollback strategy.

Performance and latency requirements: specify acceptable latency for safety-critical thresholds (<100ms target), productivity reporting (<5s target), and sampling frequency (e.g., 10Hz for volumetric flow, 1Hz for productivity). Define throughput capacity (e.g., max concurrent sensor streams, max data rate).

Network resilience strategy: if distributed acquisition is required, define fallback behavior (local caching duration, graceful degradation mode, retry logic, recovery time objective). Specify acceptable measurement gaps and data loss tolerance.

Acceptance criteria refinement: specify productivity_target value (e.g., 'reported throughput ≥ 50 m³/hour'), evaluation period duration (e.g., '8-hour operational shift'), sampling frequency (e.g., '1-minute rolling average'), and acceptable measurement uncertainty (e.g., '±5% for volumetric, ±3% for mass'). Include representative material types and flow rate ranges to be validated.

Safety-critical threshold specification: identify which measurement thresholds are safety-critical (e.g., overfill detection, flow rate limits, pressure limits) and define separate acceptance criteria with tighter tolerance and independent verification method (e.g., manual calibration check, third-party sensor comparison).
